summary: Cantrill reflects on shipping a major software release (2010.Q1) for Sun's enterprise storage product after fifteen months of intense development. He candidly acknowledges the product wasn't perfect at launch due to aggressive technology bets on flash storage and commodity components, but argues these risks were necessary to disrupt monopoly pricing in enterprise storage. The release focuses heavily on quality improvements and new features like Fibre Channel support, with Cantrill personally taking responsibility as both Technical Lead and Support Lead.
key_points: In software, you can only pick two of quality, features, and schedule - they chose quality and features
The product made multiple aggressive technology bets simultaneously because they were essential to disrupting enterprise storage pricing
Quality issues arose from hard problems, immature software, and components with unanticipated failure modes
The Technical Lead now transitions to Support Lead when shipping, creating unprecedented engineering-support integration
money_quotes: In my experience, I have found that in software (if not in life), one may only ever pick two of quality, features and schedule – and for 2010.Q1, we very much picked quality and features.

We made these bets not out of recklessness, but rather because they were essential elements of our Big Bet: that customers were sick of paying monopoly rents for enterprise storage, and that we could deliver a quantum leap in price-performance.

Sometimes the struggle was because the problem was hard, sometimes it was because the software was immature, and sometimes it was because a component that was assumed to have known failure modes had several (or many) unanticipated (or byzantine) failure modes.
We felt that expecting an SSD to have better write latency than a hard drive from the first Reagan Administration was tough but fair…
If and when you do have an issue on 2010.Q1, you should know that the buck on your support call will ultimately stop with me.
key_insight: Building truly disruptive enterprise products requires making multiple aggressive technology bets simultaneously, accepting that imperfection is inevitable when pushing boundaries, but ultimately taking personal responsibility for the outcome.
